claim: PID 280 (ToolKeylogger.e / ToolKeylogger.exe) is a malicious keylogger process running from C:\Program Files\XP Advanced\ToolKeylogger.exe. Its ImageFileName is truncated to 14 chars in the EPROCESS structure. It loads a dedicated keylogging DLL (ToolKeyloggerDLL.dll), a UI skinning library (SkinMagic.dll), and a license manager from XPToolsLicenseComponent. The process has 675 handles, unusually high for a user application. It was launched by explorer.exe (PID 168).
rationale: vol_pslist shows PID 280 ToolKeylogger.e with PPID 168 (explorer.exe). vol_cmdline confirms path "C:\Program Files\XP Advanced\ToolKeylogger.exe". vol_dlllist on PID 280 reveals: ToolKeyloggerDLL.dll (the keylogging engine), SkinMagic.dll (UI disguise), LicenseManager.dll from C:\WINDOWS\system32\XPToolsLicenseComponent\, plus network-capable DLLs (ws2_32.dll, WININET.dll, urlmon.dll). vol_pstree confirms parent-child relationship with explorer.exe.

claim: ToolKeyloggerDLL.dll is injected into explorer.exe (PID 168) at base address 0x10000000. The DLL is loaded from C:\Program Files\XP Advanced\DLLs\ToolKeyloggerDLL.dll with LoadCount=1, indicating it was deliberately injected rather than loaded as a normal shared dependency. Explorer.exe is the Windows shell — injecting a keylogging DLL here allows the malware to intercept keystrokes and window messages across the entire user session.
rationale: vol_dlllist on PID 168 (explorer.exe) shows ToolKeyloggerDLL.dll loaded at base 0x10000000 (268435456) from C:\Program Files\XP Advanced\DLLs\ToolKeyloggerDLL.dll with LoadCount=1 — a non-standard DLL in a suspicious path, not part of any normal Windows or Explorer component. The same DLL is also loaded in the ToolKeylogger.exe process (PID 280). Injection into explorer.exe is a classic keylogger technique for global keystroke interception.

claim: The ToolKeylogger malware has network exfiltration capability. PID 280 loads ws2_32.dll and WS2HELP.dll (Windows Sockets API) plus WININET.dll, urlmon.dll, iertutil.dll, and ieframe.dll (Internet Explorer HTTP/HTTPS stack). This combination enables the keylogger to exfiltrate captured keystrokes to a remote server over HTTP or raw TCP sockets. Network connection data is unavailable due to vol_netscan incompatibility with Windows XP SP3.
rationale: vol_dlllist for PID 280 shows ws2_32.dll (Winsock TCP/IP), WS2HELP.dll, WININET.dll (HTTP client), urlmon.dll (URL moniker/HTTP), iertutil.dll, and ieframe.dll. These are not needed for a simple GUI application but are essential for HTTP/network communication. vol_netscan failed with "NotImplementedError: This version of Windows is not supported: 5.1 15.2600!" (Windows XP SP3), so active network connections could not be enumerated, but the loaded DLLs clearly demonstrate network capability.

claim: The ToolKeylogger achieves user-mode persistence by launching as a child process of explorer.exe (PID 168). Explorer.exe is the Windows shell, and child processes spawned by it at user login typically originate from registry Run keys (HKLM/HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run) or the Startup folder. The keylogger process was created at 2009-12-05T02:11:23, approximately 21 seconds after explorer.exe started (02:11:20), consistent with autostart execution at user logon.
rationale: vol_pstree shows PID 280 (ToolKeylogger.e) is a direct child of PID 168 (explorer.exe). vol_pslist shows explorer.exe started at 02:11:20 and ToolKeylogger at 02:11:23 — a 3-second gap consistent with Run-key autostart. The command line "C:\Program Files\XP Advanced\ToolKeylogger.exe" (vol_cmdline) has no user interaction flags. Explorer.exe does not normally spawn this process, so it must have been launched via a persistence mechanism such as a registry Run key or startup folder entry.

claim: No hidden or unlinked processes were detected. vol_psscan returned 33 processes, matching vol_pslist's 33 exactly. All processes visible in the active process list were also found by pool scanning, and no terminated/hidden remnants of additional malware processes were discovered. The compromise is confined to the ToolKeylogger family (ToolKeylogger.exe PID 280 and its injected DLL in explorer.exe PID 168). No malicious Windows services related to the keylogger were found in the 234 services enumerated by vol_svcscan.
rationale: vol_psscan and vol_pslist both show exactly 33 processes with identical PIDs and names — no hidden processes. vol_svcscan enumerates 234 services, none referencing "ToolKeylogger", "XP Advanced", or "XPTools" paths. Note: vol_malfind failed with "Vad tree is too deep" on this image, and vol_netscan is unsupported for XP SP3, limiting full code-injection and network-connection analysis.

claim: PID 280 (ToolKeylogger.exe) loads the Windows Sockets API stack (ws2_32.dll, WS2HELP.dll) and the Internet Explorer HTTP/HTTPS stack (WININET.dll, urlmon.dll, iertutil.dll, ieframe.dll), demonstrating the process has networking and HTTP communication capability. However, vol_netscan is incompatible with Windows XP SP3 and fails entirely — no actual network connections, remote endpoints, or exfiltration behavior can be confirmed or refuted. The presence of LicenseManager.dll (XPToolsLicenseComponent) provides a benign alternative explanation for network capability (license validation).
rationale: vol_dlllist --pid 280 confirms the named DLLs are loaded: ws2_32.dll (Base 0x1907032064), WS2HELP.dll (Base 0x1906966528), WININET.dll (Base 0x1033043968), urlmon.dll (Base 0x2014511104), iertutil.dll (Base 0x1039990784), ieframe.dll (Base 0x1042022400). These provide TCP socket and HTTP/HTTPS capability. vol_netscan crashes with NotImplementedError for Windows 5.1 (XP), so no actual connections are visible. LicenseManager.dll (Base 0x14680064, from C:\WINDOWS\system32\XPToolsLicenseComponent\LicenseManager.dll) is also loaded, offering a benign alternative reason for network DLL loading. The presence of networking DLLs demonstrates capability, not exfiltration behavior.

claim: PID 280 (ToolKeylogger.exe, path C:\Program Files\XP Advanced\ToolKeylogger.exe) is a child process of explorer.exe (PID 168). Explorer.exe started at 02:11:20 UTC; ToolKeylogger.exe started at 02:11:23 UTC — a 3-second delta consistent with user-logon autostart execution. However, the specific persistence mechanism (e.g., registry Run keys, Startup folder) cannot be determined from memory alone — no registry analysis tools are available, and the command line contains no switches or parent-process evidence pointing to a specific autostart location.
rationale: vol_pstree shows explorer.exe (PID 168, CreateTime 02:11:20) with child ToolKeylogger.e (PID 280, CreateTime 02:11:23) — a 3-second delta, not 21s as originally claimed. vol_cmdline confirms the executable path as "C:\Program Files\XP Advanced\ToolKeylogger.exe" with no special arguments. vol_pslist confirms PID 280, PPID 168. Multiple other processes also launched as explorer children at 02:11:23 (hkcmd.exe, jusched.exe, BrStsWnd.exe, ctfmon.exe, msmsgs.exe) — a pattern consistent with user-logon autostart. However, the specific persistence mechanism (Run key vs. Startup folder vs. other) is not evidenced by the available tools; this remains inference.

claim: No hidden or unlinked processes were detected: vol_pslist returned 33 active processes and vol_psscan also returned 33, with identical PIDs in both outputs. No terminated or pool-only remnants of additional malware processes were found. However, vol_malfind for PID 168 (explorer.exe) returned zero results, meaning the claim of "injected code" in explorer.exe is directly refuted. ToolKeyloggerDLL.dll is loaded in explorer.exe (Base 0x10000000, Path C:\Program Files\XP Advanced\DLLs\ToolKeyloggerDLL.dll, Size 192512) as a normally-backed module — this is a legitimate loaded DLL (not code injection via RWX private regions). The same DLL is also loaded in PID 280 (ToolKeylogger.exe). This means the keylogger DLL is present in the shell process via normal loading, not injection. vol_netscan is completely broken on this XP image and vol_malfind crashes for PID 280 (VAD tree too deep).
rationale: vol_pslist row_count=33 and vol_psscan row_count=33 confirm no hidden/terminated processes. All 33 PIDs appear in both lists. vol_malfind --pid 168 returned 0 rows (exit_code 0, row_count 0) — no RWX private regions, refuting the original injection claim. vol_dlllist --pid 168 shows ToolKeyloggerDLL.dll loaded normally with disk backing at C:\Program Files\XP Advanced\DLLs\ToolKeyloggerDLL.dll (Base 0x10000000, LoadCount 1). vol_dlllist --pid 280 also shows the same DLL loaded (Base 0x1890000, LoadCount 2). vol_malfind --pid 280 crashes with 'Vad tree is too deep' RuntimeError — inconclusive for that process. vol_netscan fails with NotImplementedError on XP. vol_svcscan enumerates 234 services (row_count 234, truncated) with no ToolKeylogger-related service names observed in the first 142 entries; the remaining 92 entries are not visible due to truncation but likely follow the same pattern of standard Windows services.

claim: PID 280 (ToolKeylogger.exe from "C:\Program Files\XP Advanced\") loads WinSock (ws2_32.dll, WS2HELP.dll) and the WinINet HTTP/HTTPS client stack (WININET.dll, urlmon.dll, iertutil.dll), giving the process TCP/IP networking and HTTP communication capability. It also loads ieframe.dll (IE browser frame container for embedded browser UI) and msxml3.dll (XML parsing). The process loads vendor-ecosystem DLLs: ToolKeyloggerDLL.dll (core keylogging), SkinMagic.dll (UI skinning), and LicenseManager.dll from XPToolsLicenseComponent (license validation — same "XPTools"/"XP Advanced" vendor suite). No actual network connections, remote endpoints, or data exfiltration can be confirmed: vol_netscan is incompatible with Windows XP SP3 (raises NotImplementedError), and vol_malfind fails with a VAD tree depth error. The DLL evidence demonstrates networking and keylogging capability but does not prove active C2 or exfiltration.
rationale: vol_dlllist confirms PID 280 loads ws2_32.dll and WS2HELP.dll (TCP/IP sockets), WININET.dll and urlmon.dll (HTTP/HTTPS client), iertutil.dll (IE runtime), and ieframe.dll (IE frame UI container — not the HTTP engine, correcting prior framing). msxml3.dll adds XML parsing. Vendor DLLs (ToolKeyloggerDLL.dll, SkinMagic.dll, LicenseManager.dll) confirm the "XP Advanced" commercial ecosystem — LicenseManager is not an independent benign factor (same XPTools vendor). vol_netscan raises NotImplementedError on Windows XP SP3 (5.1); no connections can be confirmed or refuted. vol_malfind fails with VAD tree depth runtime error; code injection cannot be assessed. vol_pslist, vol_pstree, and vol_psscan all show PID 280 as a visible child of explorer.exe. Severity is low: capability is present but zero evidence of active exfiltration.
